title: Western Wildfire Support Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Western Wildfire Support Act of 2025 helps the federal government better deal with wildfires before, during, and after they happen. The bill creates special teams to help areas recover after fires burn through them and sets up funding to fix damaged land and replace important buildings. It also pays for research into new firefighting tools like drones and sensors that can spot fires early. The law requires government agencies to work together better and helps states create websites where people can find disaster help. Finally, it creates a contest to find new technology that can control harmful plants that grow after wildfires.
last_action_text: Committee on Energy and Natural Resources. Ordered to be reported with an amendment in the nature of a substitute favorably.
subjects: Social work, volunteer service, charitable organizations
Wilderness and natural areas, wildlife refuges, wild rivers, habitats

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title; Table of Contents
sections.text: This law can be called the Western Wildfire Support Act of 2025. The law has three main parts. Part One is called Preparation and covers making firefighting accounts more transparent, getting money back when military training causes fires, making strategic wildfire plans, and studying how to include local firefighters in wildfire response. Part Two is called Wildfire Detection and Suppression Support and covers putting in equipment to detect fires, buying slip-on tank units for trucks, researching fire-fighting drones, studying how private drones interfere with firefighting, and studying how to modernize fire response technology. Part Three is called Post-Fire Recovery Support and covers funding online guides to help after disasters, managing land after fires, creating a special account for long-term burned area repair, and creating a prize contest for reducing invasive species that grow after fires.

sections.title: Section 2 Definitions
sections.text: This section explains important terms used in this law. Congressional committees means the committees in the House and Senate that oversee energy, natural resources, and government spending. Federal land means public lands managed by the government, national parks, wildlife refuges, land held in trust for Indian tribes, and national forests. A fireshed is an area of forest where a fire starting anywhere in that area would threaten homes, communities, or important buildings and equipment. National Forest System means the forests managed by the Forest Service, but does not include grasslands or forests east of the one hundredth meridian (a line that runs through the middle of the United States). Resource management plan means a plan that describes how federal land will be managed. Secretaries means the Secretary of the Interior and the Secretary of Agriculture. Secretary concerned means whichever Secretary is in charge of the specific federal land being discussed.

sections.title: Section 101 Firefighting Account Transparency
sections.text: This section changes the law to require better reporting about how money is spent fighting wildfires. Each year, the government must report how much money was spent from wildfire management accounts, including any emergency money that was used. The reports must include details about catastrophic wildfires, which are fires that require federal firefighting help and either burn at least one hundred thousand acres or burn at least fifty thousand acres with half of that at high severity. These fires must also either cost at least fifty million dollars to fight, destroy at least one home, or directly cause at least one death. The reports must break down costs by ground operations, aircraft operations, personnel, support costs, administrative costs, and other relevant factors.

sections.title: Section 102 Reimbursement for Wildfires Caused by Military Training
sections.text: The Secretary of Defense must make agreements with state agencies to help each other fight fires. These agreements must include paying back state agencies for firefighting services when fires are caused by military training or other planned military actions. The payments only cover services directly related to fighting the specific fire. State agencies must provide detailed lists of what services they provided and how much they cost. The Defense Department will pay for this using money set aside for operations and maintenance. Any agreements that already exist will count as agreements under this new law as long as they meet the requirements.

sections.title: Section 103 Strategic Wildland Fire Management Planning
sections.text: By September thirtieth, twenty twenty-six, the Secretary in charge of each area must review existing fire management policies for each fireshed on federal land and create new or updated policies using the best science and planning tools available. These policies must be reviewed and updated regularly, including within one year after a wildfire happens in the area and at least once every ten years. The policies must identify potential wildfire and smoke risks to first responders, communities, important buildings and equipment, and valuable resources. They must work with any resource management plans for the area and work with states to identify potential wildfire control locations and places where firefighter risk might be higher due to steep slopes, dangerous fuel buildup, or other known hazards. The policies must also describe what weather conditions would create severe fire conditions and include other planning items as needed. When developing or updating resource management plans for federal land, the Secretary must try to include someone who helped develop the fire management policies for that fireshed on the planning team.

sections.title: Section 104 Study and Report on Integrating Local Firefighters into Wildfire Response
sections.text: Within one year of this law being passed, the Secretary of Homeland Security, working through the U.S. Fire Administration and with the National Wildfire Coordinating Group, must study the gaps in training for building firefighters in high wildfire risk areas and submit a report to Congress about the results. The report must include a summary of how federal wildland firefighters currently work with state, local, or tribal firefighters. It must analyze the differences in how state, local, or tribal firefighters respond to a fire threatening one building compared to a wildfire threatening a whole community. The report must describe existing training modules available through the National Fire Academy for training state, local, or tribal firefighters on best practices for community-threatening wildfires, or identify gaps in these training modules. It must also provide an estimated cost and spending plan to address any gaps in existing training.

sections.title: Section 201 Wildfire Detection Equipment
sections.text: As much as possible, the Secretary in charge of each area must speed up placing wildfire detection equipment like sensors, cameras, and other relevant equipment in areas at risk of wildfire. They must expand the use of satellite data to improve wildfire detection and response and speed up any permits needed for installing, maintaining, or removing wildfire detection equipment. They must use unmanned aerial vehicles (drones) to assess wildland fires in their early stages to determine the right initial response. They must review permitting and purchasing requirements for wildfire detection equipment in the context of modern and innovative technology. Each year, they must provide a forum for companies developing and testing new wildland fire technology to meet with wildland fire managers.

sections.title: Section 202 Slip-on Tanker Units
sections.text: This section changes existing law to allow Indian tribes, in addition to local governments, to receive financial help to buy firefighting slip-on tanker units (portable water tanks that can be mounted on trucks). The Secretary of the Interior must submit yearly reports to Congress about how this program is working, including the total number of slip-on tanker units bought with government help by state, the number of requests received for help, and any barriers that prevent local governments and Indian tribes from participating. The first report is due by October first, twenty twenty-six, and the reporting requirement ends on October first, twenty twenty-eight. The Secretaries, working with those who receive help buying slip-on tanker units, must work with the U.S. Fire Administration to create guidance for using slip-on tanker units in wildfire response, include them in resource tracking systems when possible, and work together on any necessary training for operators.

sections.title: Section 203 Research and Development of Unmanned Aircraft System Fire Applications
sections.text: In this section, unmanned aircraft system means an unmanned aircraft and all the parts needed to control it safely and efficiently, including communication links. The Secretaries must work through the Joint Fire Science Program with universities and other research institutions to research and develop wildfire response uses for unmanned aircraft systems. The Secretaries can work with the Federal Aviation Administration to test unmanned aircraft systems developed under this section at unmanned aircraft test ranges as allowed by federal law. Congress authorizes the Secretaries to spend whatever money is necessary to carry out this section.

sections.title: Section 204 Study on Drone Incursions on Wildfire Suppression
sections.text: In this section, Administrator means the Administrator of the Federal Aviation Administration, drone means an unmanned aircraft system owned by a private person or company, and drone incursion means operating a drone in airspace where the Administrator has issued a temporary flight restriction because of a wildfire. The Administrator, working with the Secretary of the Interior and Secretary of Agriculture through the Chief of the Forest Service, must study how drone incursions affect wildfire suppression on land managed by the Interior or Agriculture Departments. The study must determine for each of the five most recent years how many times drone incursions interfered with wildfire suppression and estimate how each incident affected the time needed to completely suppress the fire, any delays in sending out aerial firefighting units, and the amounts spent by the federal government. The study must also evaluate how feasible and effective various actions to prevent drone incursions would be, including using counter-drone radio towers, using reasonable force to disable or destroy drones, seizing drones including with net devices, and spreading educational materials about how drone incursions affect wildfire suppression. Within eighteen months of this law being passed, the Administrator must submit a report to congressional committees describing the study findings and any recommendations.

sections.title: Section 205 Study on Modernizing Wildfire Response Technologies
sections.text: The Secretaries must study necessary improvements to radio communications systems during wildland fire or prescribed fire operations. This includes assessing the quality and reliability of existing radio infrastructure, assessing the impacts when radio communications fail during forest management or wildfire response activities, comparing existing options to improve on-the-ground communications, and analyzing costs and timelines to install the most feasible option. They must also study real-time or near-real-time situational awareness tools for operational firefighters, including standards to ensure different firefighting entities can work together, requirements for additional remote sensing and mapping capabilities, and cost comparisons between commercially available systems and internally developed systems. The study must cover wildland fire predictive modeling, including analyzing data needed to reduce predictive error for existing or developing models, analyzing computing needs to more quickly or accurately model wildland fire, studying the feasibility of using artificial intelligence for wildland fire modeling, and examining existing or developing models that could help establish safe conditions for starting prescribed fires. Within one year of this law being passed, the Secretaries must submit the study results to congressional committees and make them available to the public.

sections.title: Section 301 Funding for Online Guides for Post-Disaster Assistance
sections.text: This section changes existing disaster relief law to add post-disaster assistance to the services that can be provided. It allows the Administrator of the Federal Emergency Management Agency to make agreements to provide funding or technical help to state agencies to create, update, or operate websites that provide information about post-disaster recovery funding and resources to communities or individuals affected by major disasters or emergencies. These websites must be managed by the state agency and be suitable for residents of that state. The Administrator can provide funding for websites that provide lists of federal, state, and local sources of post-disaster recovery funding for communities or individuals, or technical guides that list and explain the costs and benefits of alternatives available to communities to reduce disaster impacts and prepare for follow-up hazards like flooding after wildfires. State agencies receiving this funding must work with several federal departments and agencies when creating, updating, or operating their websites. They must update their websites at least once every one hundred eighty days.

sections.title: Section 302 Post-Fire Management and Recovery
sections.text: Within one hundred eighty days of this law being passed, the Secretary in charge of each area must establish one or more permanent Burned Area Emergency Response Teams (called BAER Teams) to coordinate immediate post-wildfire emergency stabilization and erosion planning efforts. These teams must survey landscapes affected by wildfires to determine burn severity, identify and remove dead or dying trees that may pose immediate hazards to people, communities, or critical infrastructure, and stabilize or remove hazardous materials from wildfires that may pose immediate hazards to people, communities, or the environment. They must conduct emergency landscape stabilization and erosion prevention work, prioritizing efforts that ensure public safety, prevent the spread of invasive species, help inform the public of wildfire hazards, work with activities in section three hundred three, and conduct other work as needed. BAER Teams should coordinate and respond to immediate post-wildfire emergency stabilization and erosion planning needs for no more than one year after a wildfire is contained. The Secretary can use existing Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funding to pay for this work.

sections.title: Section 303 Long-Term Burned Area Rehabilitation Account
sections.text: This section creates a new account in the U.S. Treasury called the Long-Term Burned Area Rehabilitation account for the Department of Agriculture. Congress authorizes spending up to one hundred million dollars each year starting in fiscal year twenty twenty-five to carry out rehabilitation activities. Starting in fiscal year twenty twenty-six, the Secretary of Agriculture must request money through the President's budget process for these activities. The Secretary can use this money for rehabilitation projects at sites affected by wildfires or post-wildfire flooding, primarily on federal land but also including non-federal land. These projects can restore ecosystem functions like revegetation and reforestation, watershed restoration, invasive species removal, and wildlife habitat restoration. They can also repair or replace infrastructure or facilities critical for land management activities. Rehabilitation projects must begin after a wildfire is contained and be completed within five years of containment. The Secretary can make agreements with non-federal entities to carry out these activities, with the non-federal share being no more than twenty percent and allowing in-kind contributions. The Secretary must prioritize projects nationwide based on downstream effects on water resources. Starting in fiscal year twenty twenty-seven, the Secretary must submit yearly reports to Congress on burned area recovery work performed using money from this account and other existing funding sources.

sections.title: Section 304 Prize for Wildfire-Related Invasive Species Reduction
sections.text: This section creates a new prize contest called the Theodore Roosevelt Genius Prize for the management of wildfire-related invasive species. The Secretary must establish this contest within one hundred eighty days of this law being passed to encourage technological innovation and award one or more prizes each year for technological advances that manage wildfire-related invasive species. A Management of Wildfire-Related Invasive Species Technology Advisory Board with at least nine members will be established to provide expertise in invasive species, biology, technology development, engineering, economics, business development, wildfire, and other necessary disciplines. The Board will select topics, issue problem statements, advise the Secretary about innovation opportunities, and advise winners about opportunities to pilot and implement winning technologies. The Board must consult widely with federal and non-federal stakeholders when selecting topics and issuing problem statements. The Secretary, working through the Executive Director of the National Invasive Species Council, will administer the prize contest. The Secretary will appoint at least three judges to select annual winners, though the Secretary can determine that no technological advancement merits an award in any given year. Within sixty days of awarding a cash prize, the Secretary must submit a report to Congress that includes a statement from the Board describing their activities, a description of the winners, and a statement from the judges explaining why the winners were selected. All authority under this section ends on December thirty-first, twenty twenty-eight.
